talk_title: Extracellular vesicles orchestrate pre‑metastatic niche and systemic disease

speaker.name: David Lyden
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Department of Pediatrics and Pediatric Neuro‑Oncology

targets.dependency_context: EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from brain‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from melanoma or pancreatic cancer that induce lung thrombosis
secreted by pancreatic and GI tumors influencing lung macrophages
upregulated by exomeres in liver Kupffer cells
activated T‑cell EVs delivering to tumor nuclei

biomarkers.name: β2 integrin‑positive EVs
biomarkers.finding: high levels predicted postoperative clotting in pancreatic cancer patients

biomarkers.name: CXCL13 plasma level
biomarkers.finding: elevated CXCL13 correlated with lung‑pre‑metastatic niche formation and thrombosis risk

models.name: triple‑negative breast cancer parental and organ‑tropic subclones
naive C57BL/6 mice injected with EVs
liver biopsies from pancreatic cancer patients
pancreatic cancer cohort (n≈30) with longitudinal EV profiling
models.genotype_or_key_features: tracked for clot events and fatty liver development

MOA_summary: Lyden demonstrated that tumor‑derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) carry organ‑specific integrin zip‑codes that direct EV uptake to pre‑metastatic niches; β2‑integrin‑positive EVs trigger lung thrombosis, while exomeres rich in metabolic enzymes induce fatty‑liver inflammation via Kupffer‑cell TNF‑α activation. Blocking β2‑integrin or TNF‑α mitigates these systemic effects. Activated T‑cell EVs loaded with granzyme B enter tumor nuclei, up‑regulating antigen‑presentation genes and synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 to regress pancreatic tumors.
key_findings: Integrin αVβ5‑positive EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells home to the lung and establish pre‑metastatic niches, whereas αVβ3‑positive EVs target the brain.
β2‑integrin‑bearing EVs derived from melanoma or pancreatic cancer induce rapid lung clot formation in naive mice; anti‑β2‑integrin antibody reduces fibrin deposition and improves platelet counts.
Exomeres, the smallest EV fraction, preferentially accumulate in the liver, reprogram Kupffer cells to a TNF‑α‑high, fatty‑acid‑low state, and generate fatty‑liver disease in mice.
Pharmacologic blockade of β2‑integrin or TNF‑α prevents EV‑driven thrombosis and fatty‑liver pathology and modestly delays metastatic tumor growth.

Activated T‑cell EVs package granzyme B, enter tumor cell nuclei, up‑regulate antigen‑presentation pathways, and when combined with anti‑PD‑1 produce durable tumor regressions in pancreatic cancer mouse models (median survival extended 30–40 days).
Circulating β2‑integrin‑positive EV levels prospectively predict clot events in pancreatic cancer patients, outperforming standard coagulation tests.

combination_strategies.rationale: activated T‑cell EVs increase tumor antigen presentation, potentially enhancing checkpoint blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression and extended survival in pancreatic cancer mice

combination_strategies.rationale: blocks EV‑mediated platelet binding and lung thrombosis
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced fibrin deposition and lower clot incidence in melanoma models

combination_strategies.rationale: counteracts exomere‑driven Kupffer cell inflammation and fatty‑liver development
combination_strategies.phenotype: prevention of fatty‑liver phenotype and preservation of cardiac function during chemotherapy

combination_strategies.rationale: inhibits NET formation in pre‑metastatic lung niche
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased lung thrombosis and metastasis in pancreatic cancer models

open_questions: How long do tumor‑derived EVs persist in circulation after definitive primary‑tumor resection?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which granzyme B‑loaded T‑cell EVs access the tumor cell nucleus?
Do CAF‑derived EVs differ functionally from tumor‑cell EVs in organotropism and metabolic reprogramming?
Can chronic exercise‑induced “healthy” EVs competitively displace tumor EVs in patients, and what biomarkers best capture this shift?
